topic: Area of Study 3: Music for Film
subject: music

items.q: What does modulation mean in music?
items.correct: Changing from one key to another within a piece.
items.why: Students conflate modulation with any musical change. Specifically, modulation is a KEY CHANGE, usually established by a perfect cadence in the new key - a structural device.

items.q: What happens in the recapitulation of sonata form?
items.correct: Both subjects return, but the second subject is now in the tonic key.
items.why: Students miss the KEY RESOLUTION. The exposition put subject 2 in a contrasting key; the recapitulation brings it back to the tonic, resolving the tonal tension - this is the dramatic point of sonata form.

items.q: Which orchestral family does the saxophone belong to?
items.misconception: Brass (because it is made of brass).
items.why: Students classify by material. Instrument families are defined by SOUND PRODUCTION: woodwinds use a reed or air across an edge. The sax uses a reed, so despite the brass body, it's woodwind.

items.q: Which composer wrote the leitmotif-based score for 'Star Wars' (1977)?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: John Williams. “Hans Zimmer” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What term describes music that originates within the film world (e.g. a character playing piano)?
items.why: Correct answer: Source music. “Underscore” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was John Williams's 'Star Wars' score released?
items.why: The real figure is 1977. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
